title: Te Whakapakari Tinana me te Hauora o Te Awa: Strengthening Body and Spirit Through Our River
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This unit connects to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway flowing through our rohe that holds deep cultural, spiritual, and nutritional significance for Ngāti Kahungunu. Students will explore how our tūpuna maintained physical and spiritual health through their intimate relationship with the awa, using its resources for healing, sustenance, and physical conditioning while practicing kaitiakitanga.
learningObjectives: Analyze how traditional practices along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku promoted holistic hauora (physical, mental, spiritual, and social wellbeing)
Design and participate in a modified traditional physical activity inspired by mahinga kai practices specific to our awa
Create a personal wellness plan that incorporates at least two traditional Ngāti Kahungunu health practices connected to our local environment
Collaboratively develop and present a proposal for enhancing student wellbeing at Mangakōtukutuku College using traditional knowledge

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa: Connecting to Our River
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua to learn traditional narratives, identify native species with medicinal or nutritional value (such as kōura, watercress, and specific rongoā plants), and understand the river's role in maintaining community hauora throughout generations.

activities.title: Mahi Tinana o Mua: Traditional Physical Practices
activities.description: Students participate in hands-on sessions learning modified versions of traditional physical activities connected to the awa, including waka ama paddling techniques (using land-based simulators), simulated tuna (eel) harvesting movements, and carrying water in tīheru (calabashes) to develop functional strength.

activities.title: Rongoā Māori: Traditional Healing Practices
activities.description: Working with local healers, students identify, harvest (with permission), and prepare simple rongoā from plants found along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, learning about their traditional uses for physical recovery, mental clarity, and spiritual balance while discussing modern applications.

activities.title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardian Project
activities.description: Students work in small groups to assess a section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, documenting its current state and proposing one practical enhancement that would improve both the health of the awa and the wellbeing of the school community, connecting environmental health to human health.

resources: Local kaumātua and knowledge holders from Ngāti Kahungunu
Mangakōtukutuku College's 'Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Cultural Health Assessment' document
Traditional implements: tīheru (water carriers), modified waka ama paddles, tuna pots
Digital access to 'Ngā Rongoā o Te Awa' plant identification guide developed by local marae

tasks: Create an illustrated journal documenting three traditional health practices connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and their modern applications
Design and lead a 10-minute physical activity session for classmates based on traditional movements
Develop and present a 'Hauora Enhancement Proposal' for the school incorporating traditional knowledge

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A1 Personal Health and Physical Development, B3 Relationships with Other People, C3 Societal Attitudes and Values, D3 Healthy Communities and Environments
Tikanga ā-Iwi: Understanding how cultural practices and principles contribute to healthy communities
Te Reo Māori: Using language related to hauora, tinana, and wairua in authentic contexts
